mockBank.explanation: South Asians develop Type 2 diabetes at lower BMI values than White Europeans because they tend to have higher visceral (abdominal) fat relative to subcutaneous fat at any given BMI. Visceral fat is more metabolically active and causes greater insulin resistance. NICE recommends screening South Asian, Black African, and Black Caribbean people for Type 2 diabetes at a BMI threshold of 27.5 kg/m² rather than the 30 kg/m² used for White Europeans.

mockBank.question: When investigating Cushing's syndrome, which ONE of the following initial screening test result would be EXPECTED?
options: A. Low serum cortisol on morning measurement
B. Failure to suppress cortisol after an overnight low-dose dexamethasone suppression test
C. Elevated plasma ACTH with suppressed serum cortisol
D. Normal 24-hour urinary free cortisol
E. Suppressed cortisol after high-dose dexamethasone suppression test
mockBank.explanation: The low-dose dexamethasone suppression test (LDDST) is used to screen for Cushing's syndrome: 1 mg dexamethasone is given at midnight and cortisol measured at 9 am. In a normal person, dexamethasone (a synthetic corticosteroid) suppresses the HPA axis, causing cortisol to fall below 50 nmol/L. In Cushing's syndrome, excess cortisol production is not suppressed — a positive test (failure to suppress). 24-hour urinary free cortisol is another screening test, and would be ELEVATED (not normal) in Cushing's.

mockBank.question: Microalbuminuria is an early marker of diabetic nephropathy. Which ONE of the following BEST describes why this occurs?
options: A. Diabetes causes kidney stones that obstruct urinary flow
B. Chronic hyperglycaemia damages the glomerular filtration barrier, allowing albumin to leak into the urine
C. Diabetic patients have high protein intake causing proteinuria
D. ACE inhibitors used in diabetes reduce albumin reabsorption
E. Hyperinsulinaemia increases glomerular pressure causing filtration of albumin
mockBank.explanation: Diabetic nephropathy begins with microalbuminuria — small amounts of albumin leaking into the urine — due to damage to the glomerular filtration barrier from chronic hyperglycaemia. AGEs (advanced glycation end-products) damage the glomerular basement membrane and mesangium. Over time, this progresses to overt proteinuria, declining GFR, and end-stage renal disease. Early detection and treatment with ACE inhibitors (which reduce intra-glomerular pressure) can slow progression. ACE inhibitors are thus both renoprotective and cardioprotective in diabetics.

mockBank.question: A patient is taking prednisolone 5 mg daily for polymyalgia rheumatica. For a routine dental extraction under local anaesthesia, which ONE of the following statements about steroid cover is CORRECT according to current guidance?
options: A. All patients on any dose of steroid require 100 mg IM hydrocortisone before dental extraction
B. Patients on prednisolone ≤5 mg/day are unlikely to have significant HPA suppression and can usually proceed with their normal dose without additional cover
C. Prednisolone must be stopped 2 weeks before any dental procedure
D. Dental extractions under LA are too stressful for any steroid-dependent patient
E. The patient needs to switch to IV hydrocortisone before all dental procedures
mockBank.explanation: Current evidence suggests that patients on prednisolone ≤5 mg/day (physiological replacement dose) for longer than 4 weeks may not have significant HPA axis suppression for minor procedures under LA. They can usually proceed with their normal dose. However, for moderate/major surgical stress, doubling the dose or supplementing with hydrocortisone may be appropriate. Higher doses (prednisolone >10 mg/day) carry significantly higher HPA suppression risk. The decision must be individualised and the GP/specialist consulted if uncertain.

mockBank.question: Hypothyroidism in adults, if untreated, may progress to myxoedema coma. Which ONE of the following BEST describes this emergency?
options: A. Thyroid storm with extreme hyperthyroidism and hyperpyrexia
B. Life-threatening hypothyroidism with altered consciousness, hypothermia, bradycardia, hypoventilation, and hypotension
C. Addisonian crisis with hypoglycaemia and hypotension
D. Pituitary apoplexy with sudden severe headache and visual loss
E. Diabetic ketoacidosis with acidosis and hyperglycaemia
mockBank.explanation: Myxoedema coma is an extreme, life-threatening manifestation of severe hypothyroidism, typically precipitated by infection, cold exposure, or drugs (sedatives, opioids) in an elderly, untreated hypothyroid patient. Features include: depressed consciousness/coma, profound hypothermia, bradycardia, hypoventilation, hyponatraemia, and hypoglycaemia. Mortality is high (30–60%). Treatment is IV thyroxine (T4) or liothyronine (T3), IV hydrocortisone (in case of concurrent adrenal insufficiency), and supportive care.

mockBank.question: Pregnancy causes changes in glucose metabolism. Which ONE of the following BEST explains gestational diabetes?
options: A. Pre-existing Type 1 diabetes that becomes symptomatic during pregnancy
B. Insulin resistance caused by placental hormones (e.g. human placental lactogen) that exceeds the pancreas's ability to compensate
C. Autoimmune beta-cell destruction triggered by foetal antigens
D. Excessive dietary carbohydrate intake during pregnancy
E. SGLT-2 upregulation in the kidneys causing glucose loss
mockBank.explanation: Gestational diabetes develops when the insulin resistance of pregnancy — caused by counter-regulatory hormones from the placenta (especially human placental lactogen, progesterone, and cortisol) — exceeds the pancreatic beta-cells' capacity to compensate with increased insulin secretion. It typically resolves after delivery but significantly increases the lifetime risk of developing Type 2 diabetes. Risk factors include obesity, family history, and South Asian ethnicity. Management involves diet, metformin, and insulin if needed.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following describes the clinical presentation that should prompt investigation for a phaeochromocytoma?
options: A. Young woman with weight gain, cold intolerance, and bradycardia
B. Middle-aged man with episodic severe headaches, profuse sweating, palpitations, and postural hypertension
C. Elderly woman with polyuria, constipation, and bone pain
D. Young man with moon face, central obesity, and skin striae
E. Patient with fatigue, hyperpigmentation, and postural hypotension
mockBank.explanation: A phaeochromocytoma — adrenal medullary tumour secreting catecholamines — classically presents with episodic (or sustained) hypertension, severe headache, profuse sweating, palpitations, and pallor. Postural hypotension may also occur. It can be precipitated by surgery, anaesthesia, or even a blood pressure cuff. Key investigation: 24-hour urine or plasma metanephrines. Failure to diagnose pre-operatively can lead to fatal hypertensive crisis during anaesthesia. Option E describes Addison's disease.
